items.title: Cuba under pressure as mounting sanctions spark fears of humanitarian emergency | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The United Nations says it is deeply alarmed by the worsening crisis in Cuba, as the Caribbean island struggles under a de facto US blockade on oil shipments. Cuba is running out of fuel amid mounting U.S. pressure, having last received a delivery from Mexico. The shortage has forced several airlines to cancel all flights to the island, dealing a major blow to tourism — the country’s main source of foreign currency.

items.title: Can Europe and MAGA coexist? | Berlin Briefing Podcast live from MSC 2026
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Did US Secretary of State Marco Rubio just send Europe a political Valentine's card? And after all the ups and downs, are Europeans ready to rebuild trust in their longtime partner? How do Germany and its European allies protect their interests and wield their leverage in a multipolar world, in which they depend on both the US and China? 
In a special episode recorded live at the Munich Security Conference, Berlin Briefing hosts Michaela Küfner and Nina Haase dissect the Merz government’s approach with CDU/CSU Chief Whip Jens Spahn and the Eurasia Group’s Ian Bremmer.

CHAPTERS:
00:00 Intro 
00:41 Rubio reaction
04:36 A sustainable Ukraine peace?
08:48 Painful compromises 
12:06 Germany needs economic growth
15:17 Rebuilding US-European trust 
18:17 China tensions  
21:32 Europe’s leverage 
24:26 An uncomfortable choice 
30:05 Common dreams, different nightmares 
30:35 Family analogies 
32:27 A nuanced transatlantic approach 
35:58 Multipolar rocket fuel 
37:52 Trump's lasting impact 
40:07 A look into the future 
44:08 Outro

items.title: Surviving the flames: One year after California's firestorm, communities rebuild | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>One year after firestorms tore through Southern California, Deutsche Welle’s Ines Pohl and Nils Hünerfürst return to the places forever changed - Altadena and the Palisades.

In “Surviving the Flames: Stories of Strength and Community,” they meet families still living on the lots where their homes once stood, volunteers rebuilding entire neighborhoods by hand, and innovators designing homes that can withstand the next inferno.

This is a story of heartbreak - and of humanity. It’s about how people come together when everything familiar is gone, and what it takes to prepare for a future where wildfires are no longer the exception, but the rule. 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: 250,000 rally against Iran's regime as world leaders gather at Munich Security Conference | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Munich police say about 250,000 people joined a rally protesting against the Islamic Republic of Iran, as world leaders gathered nearby at the city's Security Conference.

Protesters condemned last month’s deadly crackdown on nationwide demonstrations in Iran, which human rights groups say left thousands dead. Some carried banners echoing pro‑Trump slogans and held placards supporting the exiled son of former Shah Reza Pahlavi, who has urged the US president to support the Iranian people at the conference.

items.title: Trump prepares US military for sustained operation in Iran
items.author: Jonathan Rugman
items.content:
The US military is preparing for the possibility of sustained, weeks-long operations against Iran if President Donald Trump orders an attack.

The disclosure by US officials to the Reuters news agency came as a second American aircraft carrier was sent to the region and raises the stakes for the diplomacy underway between the two nations.

US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner will hold negotiations with Iran on Tuesday in Geneva, with representatives from Oman acting as mediators.

items.title: Important world ‘keeps pressure on Russia, not Ukraine’ – Estonian PM
items.author: Lindsey Hilsum
items.content: We spoke with the Estonian prime minister Kristen Michal and asked him how he felt about Marco Rubio’s speech to European leaders in Munich, and the failure of the US secretary of state to attend a security conference meeting on Ukraine.

items.title: European Leaders greet Rubio’s transatlantic message with relief
items.author: Lindsey Hilsum
items.content:
Sir Keir Starmer has used the Munich Security Conference to signal a willingness for the UK to have a closer defence relationship with EU nations, saying we are not the Britain of the Brexit years anymore.

His comments followed US Secretary of State Marco Rubio insisting that America and Europe “belong together” but warning that Washington will not be a “caretaker of the West’s managed decline.”

items.title: Russian opposition leader Navalny poisoned by Kremlin, five European nations say | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Five European countries have accused Russia's Kremlin of using a rare frog poison to kill opposition leader Alexei Navalny in an Arctic prison two years ago.

The staunch critic of Russian President Vladimir Putin was serving a 19-year-sentence. Britain, France, Germany, Sweden and the Netherlands say samples from Navalny's body revealed a toxin found in the skin of dart frogs native to South America. The European nations say it's 'highly likely' this resulted in his death. The Russian government ​has denied any responsibility for Navalny's death.

00:00 Navalny poisoned by Kremlin
00:38 Marc-Michael Blum, Biochemist, Blum Scientific

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Is Trump’s Manufacturing Comeback Real?
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:
<article>Steve Rattner of Willett Advisors examines whether tariffs and trade policy are actually changing the trajectory of US manufacturing. He discusses the data behind industrial output, the consumer cost of keeping out Chinese EV maker BYD, and the strategic trade-offs of protecting domestic industry. As President Trump touts potential for 15% GDP growth, Rattner explains why that’s unlikely to happen — and why productivity gains from AI may matter more than tariffs.
--------

items.title: MSC 2026: Gaza peace plan and Hamas I Conflict Zone
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>According to media reports, an American led draft proposal for the next phase of the Gaza ceasefire would allow Hamas to retain some of its weapons. Nickolay Mladenov, executive member of Trump’s Board of Peace and High Representative for Gaza, did not comment on the reports during a high level debate at the Munich Security Conference.
In this special edition of Conflict Zone, DW’s Sarah Kelly moderated a discussion on Trump’s vision for a “New Gaza.” The panel brought together the foreign ministers of the Palestinian Authority and Spain, senior representatives from the U.S and the UAE, as well as High Representative for Gaza. They debated Gaza’s future—addressing the humanitarian crisis, security challenges, the question of Hamas disarmament, and the broader path toward a two state solution.
#MSC2026 #Gaza #ConflictZone

items.title: ‘This is an engagement Europe should accept’: German FM praises Rubio speech at MSC 2026 | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul said US Secretary of State Marco Rubio had delivered a "very positive speech underlining our common basis" at the 2026 Munich Security Conference. Wadephul said Rubio had pointed to the long success story of the transatlantic partnership and his key message was: "Let’s do it again."  
Europe should take the offer, the German Foreign Minister emphasized, and consider its own contribution to the success of the transatlantic alliance. 
Wadephul said that European countries – including Germany – and the United States were "reading from the same page" with regard to threats lying ahead, even if their policy priorities didn’t completely overlap. Wadephul said Rubio’s speech was "a different category" than the controversial address delivered by US Vice President JD Vance at the 2025 conference, which had surprised and rattled many attendees.  
Asked if a more conciliatory tone from the US side could run the risk of some European countries once again slowing down their efforts to increase investment into defense, the German FM said: "We can't allow anybody to go back to sleep, because the threats are there."  
On Ukraine, the US and European positions were aligned, Wadephul said, pointing to Rubio's speech: “He said, and this is also our position, that we have to put more pressure on Russia as the United States is doing, in their talks, and in their negotiations with India, so Putin really feels economic pressure."

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Why The Ultra Rich Are Moving to Milan
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:
<article>Milan’s hosting of the Winter Olympics comes as the city is emerging as a new hub for global wealth. Originally driven by Italy’s favorable flat-tax regime, the city is now benefiting from stability, expanding financial activity and a cosmopolitan lifestyle that it hopes can rival London and Paris. Yet rising property prices and mounting cost-of-living concerns highlight the challenges that come with rapid growth. The real test for Milan may be finding a balance between prosperity and authenticity.
--------

items.title: Polish parliament approves legal amnesty for Poles fighting in Ukraine’s armed forces
items.author: Daniel Tilles
items.content:
Keep our news free from ads and paywalls by making a donation to support our work!

Notes from Poland is run by a small editorial team and is published by an independent, non-profit foundation that is funded through donations from our readers. We cannot do what we do without your support.

Polish lawmakers have approved granting legal amnesty to Poles who have taken up arms in defence of Ukraine. Normally, serving in a foreign military without consent from the Polish authorities is a crime punishable by up to five years in prison.

On Friday, the proposed new “law on the non-punishment of volunteers defending the freedom and independence of Ukraine” was supported almost unanimously by the Sejm, the more powerful lower house of parliament. Only the far-right did not vote in favour.

Polish volunteers fighting for Ukraine “deserve praise, not the threat of punishment,” declared one of the originators of the bill, Małgorzata Tracz, in the Sejm.

The bill, which was first proposed by MPs from the centrist Civic Coalition (KO), Poland’s main ruling party, in December 2024, would provide immunity for Poles who have joined the Ukrainian armed forces.

The initial bill was intended to cover those who have served since Russia launched its full-scale invasion in February 2022. However, the latest version covers the period from April 2014, when Russian-backed separatists began the war in Donbas against the Ukrainian government.

Polish citizens or foreigners residing in Poland who have served in the Armed Forces of Ukraine during that period would be granted immunity from prosecution on the condition that they notify the defence ministry upon their return to Poland.

Those who have already been sentenced for fighting in Ukraine’s armed forces are also granted an amnesty. It is not known how many such convictions have been issued, as no such statistics are kept, notes news service WNP.

The authors of the bill argue that, “in the existing international context, the participation of a Polish citizen in Ukraine’s fight against Russian aggression cannot be perceived as a violation of the constitutional obligations of loyalty to Poland, concern for the common good, or obligation to defend the homeland”.

Work on the bill involved a rare degree of bipartisanship among Poland’s often deeply divided political parties. That was reflected in the vote in the Sejm, where the bill was supported by both the ruling coalition – which stretches from left to centre right – and the main national-conservative opposition party, Law and Justice (PiS).

However, all MPs present from the far-right Confederation (Konfederacja) group abstained from voting, while all three lawmakers from the radical-right Confederation of the Polish Crown (KKP) party led by Grzegorz Braun voted against the bill. Both those groups are critical of Ukraine.

The bill now moves to the upper-house Senate, which can delay legislation and suggest amendments, but cannot block it.

Once approved by parliament, it would then pass to President Karol Nawrocki, who can sign it into law, veto it, or send it to the constitutional court for assessment.

Nawrocki has not publicly taken a position on the bill. He was elected to the presidency last year with the support of PiS but also enjoys good relations with Confederation, and has sometimes taken a tougher line on Ukraine than PiS. However, the president is also clear that Ukraine must be supported against Russia.

Andrzej Podgórski, spokesman for the so-called Polish Legion, an organisation of Polish volunteers fighting for Ukraine, says that its members have been pushing for such a law for a long time.

“Many of these volunteers who are fighting for Ukraine, besides the daily challenges of just surviving from day to day, also have this worry about what will happen when I return home,” he said, quoted by news website Gazeta.pl. “It’s important to provide them with this psychological peace of mind.”

There are no precise data on how many Poles have fought in Ukraine, but Gazeta.pl cites estimates that the proposed law would benefit several hundred people.

Notes from Poland is run by a small editorial team and published by an independent, non-profit foundation that is funded through donations from our readers. We cannot do what we do without your support.

Daniel Tilles is editor-in-chief of . He has written on Polish affairs for a wide range of publications, including , ,  and .

items.title: Marco Rubio at MSC: Europe's destiny 'intertwined' with US's
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US Secretary of State Marco Rubio has told leaders at the Munich Security Conference that Europe and the US "belong together." Rubio spoke of a shared future for the transatlantic alliance, saying that the US doesn't want to separate from Europe but it wants to "revitalize" the relationship. Rubio's speech came a year after Vice President JD Vance used the same stage to attack European policies on immigration and free speech. With a decidedly different tone, Rubio still emphasized what he said was the need for changes in the relationship.

items.title: MSC 2026: Debate on Gaza's future I Conflict Zone
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The Palestinian Authority’s Foreign Minister, Varsen Aghabekian, calls for Palestinian self determination and emphasizes that “Gaza is not separable from the West Bank.” She took part in a high level panel at this year’s Munich Security Conference, where DW’s Sarah Kelly moderated a discussion on Trump's vision for a 'New Gaza'. The panel brought together the foreign ministers of the Palestinian Authority and Spain, senior representatives from the U.S. and the UAE, as well as the High Representative for Gaza, Nickolay Mladenov. They debated the future of Gaza—addressing the humanitarian crisis, security concerns, the disarmament of Hamas, and the broader path toward a two state solution.

#MSC2026 #Gaza #ConflictZone
